concise:
Ayah 1–14 · linked video: 01. Ad-Dukhan (Ayah 1-14)

Surah Ad-Dukhan: Ayahs 1-14

Ayah 1

حمٓ

The Surah opens with the mysterious letters Ha-Meem. This Surah is part of a connected series—all the Ha-Meem Surahs share similar literary structures and themes, beginning with these distinct letters and culminating in Surah Al-Ahqaf. These letters draw our attention and prepare us for the profound truths about to be revealed.

Ayah 2

وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

“By the Book that is clear and clarifying.” Allah ﷻ takes an oath by the Quran itself. In classical Arabic, an oath serves two purposes: to capture the listener’s attention and to prepare them for an important point about to be made. The word المبين carries a dual meaning—it is both clear in itself and clarifying for others, illuminating the path for those who seek guidance.

Ayah 3

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنذِرِينَ

“Indeed, We sent it down in a blessed night. Indeed, We have always been the ones who warn.”

This ayah reveals something remarkable about the revelation. The word أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ) uses the form of the verb that indicates immediate descent, as opposed to تنزيل , which implies a gradual process. This linguistic precision points to an important reality: the Quran’s revelation occurred in two stages. First, the entire Quran was sent down from Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz (the Preserved Tablet) in the seventh heaven to the first heaven in one blessed night. Then, over twenty-three years, it was gradually revealed to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ through the Angel Jibreel (AS).

The night is described as مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ —blessed. The concept of barakah encompasses two beautiful meanings: first, it produces more good than expected, and second, it draws out dormant goodness from within. Laylatul Qadr, the Night of Power in Ramadan, possesses both these qualities. Everything you do in it multiplies in reward beyond expectation, and it awakens spiritual goodness within you that may have been lying dormant all year. This is why the Prophet ﷺ urged us to seek out this night vigilantly—its effects can impact the entire year ahead, even your entire life.

Allah ﷻ states, “We have always been the ones who warn.” This establishes both the source and the timing—the warning comes from the Most Powerful source at the most blessed time.

Ayah 4

فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ

“In it every matter of wisdom is decreed.”

The word يُفْرَقُ means to separate, distinguish, or decree. This reveals another dimension of Laylatul Qadr—it’s not merely the night the Quran was revealed, but a night when all of Allah’s decrees for the coming year are decided. Like a government’s budget session where major decisions are planned and allocated, but unlike any human planning session that gets postponed or revised, Allah’s wisdom completes all decisions in this one blessed night.

What gets decided? Who will live and who will die. Who will fall ill and who will be healed. Whether a boy or girl will be born. Which nations will receive rain and which will face drought. Every matter that affects our lives in the coming year is decreed in this night—and every decree is حَكِيمٍ (hakeem), full of wisdom.

This reality carries profound implications for us. If we invest these nights wisely with sincere worship and dua, we position ourselves for a blessed year ahead. The difficulties that might be destined for us can be changed through dua—this is the only thing that can alter divine decree. These nights are when decrees are written, so our prayers during them can influence what our year will look like. This is why these nights demand our utmost seriousness and devotion.

Ayah 5

أَمْرًا مِّنْ عِندِنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ

“A command from Us. Indeed, We have always been the ones who send.”

This is a أَمْرًا —a command, a verdict that comes specifically from Allah ﷻ. The phrase إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (We have always been the ones who send) reveals an important truth: Laylatul Qadr didn’t begin with the revelation of the Quran. This blessed night has always existed, and Allah ﷻ has always been sending down His angels on this night to deliver His verdicts. The special nature of this night is eternal, part of Allah’s continuous mercy to His creation.

Ayah 6

رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

“As mercy from your Master. Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.”

The revelation, the blessed night, the decrees—all of this is رَحْمَةً , an act of extraordinary mercy from your Rabb. The grammatical form emphasizes the magnitude of this mercy—it is profound, encompassing, overwhelming in its generosity.

Allah ﷻ concludes by reminding us: He is السَّمِيعُ , the All-Hearing of every prayer whispered in these blessed nights, and العَلِيمُ , the All-Knowing of every intention in every heart. He hears your dua and knows what you truly need, even before you ask.

Ayah 7

رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ

“Master of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them—if you would be convinced.”

Allah ﷻ identifies Himself as the Rabb—the Master, Sustainer, and Controller—of all the heavens, the earth, and everything in between. This declaration comes with a condition: إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (if you would be convinced).

Some scholars note that this ayah was revealed during Ramadan, and there’s wisdom in this timing. One of Ramadan’s great blessings is that the devils are chained, their whispers silenced. Throughout the year, shaytan works to plant doubt, to distract from truth, to make serious matters seem trivial. But in Ramadan, particularly in these blessed nights, those barriers are removed. The offer to recognize Allah ﷻ as Master is renewed when minds are clearest and hearts are most receptive. The distractions of devils and their whispers cannot interfere—you can listen and contemplate without those obstacles.

This is your opportunity to be among the موقنين —those who have certainty, those who are convinced beyond doubt.

Ayah 8

لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

“There is no deity except Him; He gives life and causes death—your Master and the Master of your earliest ancestors.”

The fundamental declaration of faith: لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ —there is no one worthy of worship and obedience in any way, shape, or form except Him alone. He is the One who يُحْىِۦ (gives life) and يُمِيتُ (causes death)—the two most profound transitions of human existence are entirely in His control.

He is not just your Rabb, but رَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (the Master of your earliest ancestors). This connects you to every generation that came before, establishing that His authority and lordship extend across all of human history. Your ancestors knew this truth, were called to this truth—and now it reaches you.

Ayah 9

بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ

“Rather, they are in doubt, playing around.”

After presenting such clear truth, Allah ﷻ exposes the real problem: بَلْ —rather, instead of accepting this clear reality, they are فِى شَكٍّ (in doubt). The word شَكٍّ indicates uncertainty, questioning, lack of conviction.

But here’s what’s particularly troubling: the combination of doubt with يَلْعَبُونَ (playing around). When someone has genuine doubt about something serious, logic dictates they should investigate, verify, seek answers. If you heard there might be an earthquake coming, even if uncertain, you wouldn’t joke about it—you’d find out for sure. But this describes a different attitude entirely: they’re not even bothering to verify. They’re treating the matter as entertainment, as idle conversation, as something not worth taking seriously.

The topics being discussed are grave—hellfire, paradise, the Day of Judgment, eternal salvation. Yet because they allow doubt to persist, they treat these weighty matters as fiction, as amusing conversation, just blowing hot air. The seriousness completely evaporates. This is not using one’s intellect responsibly.

Ayah 10

فَٱرْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِدُخَانٍ مُّبِينٍ

“Then watch for the Day when the sky will bring clear smoke.”

The Prophet ﷺ is told: فَٱرْتَقِبْ —wait in anticipation, watch carefully. The word ارتقاب carries the sense of vigilant watching, like someone monitoring election results minute by minute or following a crucial sports match second by second, constantly checking for updates. The Prophet ﷺ is told to watch for a specific event: when the sky attacks with دُخَانٍ مُّبِينٍ (clear, obvious smoke).

The word دُخَان specifically refers to a reddish or dark-textured smoke that results from drought and famine conditions. Scholars of the first generation held different views about what this smoke represents. Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA) believed it refers to the Day of Judgment, when the entire world will be covered in smoke—oceans boiling, buildings leveled, mountains crumbling, all producing massive clouds of smoke covering the earth.

However, Abdullah ibn Mas’ud (RA) held that this refers to a severe famine that struck Makkah during the Prophet’s ﷺ time. The drought became so extreme that dust storms covered the city, the dry earth rising in the air, creating a smoke-like haze that wouldn’t dissipate. The land dried completely, and the dirt-filled air covered everything like smoke.

The second interpretation gains strength when we consider the broader context. Throughout these Surahs, the story of Musa (AS) and Fir’awn has appeared repeatedly. Musa (AS) was given nine signs—nine warnings—as Allah says elsewhere: “So We sent upon them various punishments so they might humble themselves.” These were lesser punishments before the ultimate punishment, opportunities to turn back to Allah. This smoke upon Makkah may well have been such a sign for the Quraysh—a chance to recognize the truth before facing greater consequences.

Ayah 11

يَغْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ ۖ هَـٰذَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

“Covering the people. This is a painful punishment!”

The smoke يَغْشَى —covers, envelops, overwhelms the people completely. The people themselves will look at it and declare: هَـٰذَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (This is a painful punishment). The word أَلِيم doesn’t just mean painful—it means deeply, intensely painful, affecting them to their core.

Ayah 12

رَّبَّنَا ٱكْشِفْ عَنَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ

“Our Master! Remove the punishment from us; indeed, we are believers!”

In their desperation, they cry out: رَّبَّنَا —Our Master! Notice they suddenly acknowledge Him as Rabb when punishment strikes. اكْشِفْ عَنَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ (Remove this punishment from us!) And then the promise: إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ (We are believers! We will believe!)

This echoes exactly what happened with Fir’awn and his chiefs. When the plagues struck Egypt, they came to Musa (AS): “Ask your Lord to remove this from us, and we will believe in you.” But what happened after each plague was lifted? They returned to their disbelief.

This pattern appears at both national and personal levels. Consider someone on a plane during severe turbulence, or on a boat in a storm. Look around and you’ll see people praying, making promises to God—regardless of their religion or beliefs. Even atheists cry out, “Oh God, please!” in those moments. “I’m sorry, I won’t do it again, just give me another chance!”

But then when the turbulence passes, when the pilot apologizes and things return to normal, what happens? Everyone plugs their earphones back in, returns to their movies and games. Life goes back to normal. The promises evaporate.

Ayah 13

أَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ وَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ

“How can there be a reminder for them when a clear Messenger has already come to them?”

Allah ﷻ asks rhetorically: أَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ (How can there be a reminder for them? What’s the point of the reminder for them now?) This faith born of desperation—what value does it have?

The crucial point: وَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (when a clear Messenger has already come to them). A رَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (Rasul Mubeen)—a Messenger who is himself clear and who clarifies the message—has already been sent. If they were truly interested in guidance, the Messenger would have been enough. They didn’t need a natural disaster or punishment to believe. The truth was already delivered clearly.

These people aren’t looking for guidance. They’re looking for an escape from immediate pain.

Ayah 14

ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ

“Then they turned away from him and said, ‘One taught by others, a madman.’”

Once the crisis passes, تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ (they turn away from him)—they turn their backs on the Messenger. And then come the allegations: مُعَلَّمٌ —“He’s just been taught! He’s plagiarizing! He took bits from the Bible, pieces from Greek stories, mashed it all together. It’s not original.”

And if that doesn’t work to discredit him: مَّجْنُونٌ —“He’s crazy! He’s insane! He’s possessed by jinn!”

Notice something critical: these two allegations are completely contradictory. If someone is insane, if their intellect is overtaken by madness or possession, they cannot be taught. You cannot teach a madman. Yet they throw both accusations together, one after the other.

Why this contradiction? They’re desperate. They’re grasping at straws, throwing anything that might stick, anything that might prevent people from listening to the Prophet ﷺ. They know him—they’ve known him for forty years. They’ve sought his counsel in serious political and economic matters. They’ve trusted his judgment, his character, his wisdom. They know he’s not insane. They know he doesn’t compose poetry or fabricate stories.

Yet when he begins reciting these ayahs, one after another, each more powerful than the last, they panic: “Where is he getting this from?” So they frantically accuse: “He must be copying from somewhere! No, wait—he’s possessed! He’s crazy!”

Earlier in the Quran, Allah ﷻ already refuted this: “Your companion is not insane.” The one who has been in your life, your business partner, your adviser—suddenly he’s insane? It doesn’t add up, and they know it.

And yes, the Prophet ﷺ was مُعَلَّمٌ (taught)—but taught by whom? By Jibreel (AS), by divine revelation. There’s an element of truth in their accusation, but they attribute the teaching to the wrong source, deliberately ignoring the obvious truth staring them in the face.

Ayah 14–38 · linked video: 02. Ad-Dukhan (Ayah 14-38)

Surah Ad-Dukhan: Ayahs 14-38 Commentary

Ayah 14

ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ

The Quraysh experienced something unprecedented when they heard the Quran. The rhyme scheme hit them like nothing else they had encountered. Arab poets would recite their verses, pausing to remember their lines, but the Quran just kept flowing, ayah after ayah, without any interruption. The succession of ideas and the powerful rhythm scheme overwhelmed them completely.

They couldn’t comprehend what was happening. This man they had known their entire lives never spoke like this before. In their confusion and denial, they turned away from the Prophet ﷺ and declared him “mu’allam” (taught by someone) and “majnun” (possessed). They couldn’t accept that this eloquence came from Allah ﷻ, so they attributed it to either human instruction or madness.

Ayah 15

إِنَّا كَاشِفُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابِ قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ عَآئِدُونَ

Allah ﷻ responds to their earlier plea during the smoke that afflicted them. When the punishment came, they cried out: “Our Master, remove the punishment from us and we will believe!” But Allah ﷻ already knows the truth: “We are going to alleviate the punishment just a little bit. Indeed, you will return to your former ways.”

This raises an important question: if Allah ﷻ already knows they won’t truly change, why lift the punishment at all? The answer reveals a profound principle of divine justice. This is actually a form of punishment for the truly wicked. The more truth someone is exposed to, the more accountable they become. When they reject that truth, their liability on the Day of Judgment increases.

Consider the levels of accountability: Someone never exposed to revelation is asked on Judgment Day about reflecting on creation - the sky, mountains, and trees. Those who received messengers face the question: “Didn’t a warner come to you?” Those who heard the Book of Allah ﷻ recited face even greater accountability. And now, the Quraysh experienced the smoke, turned to Allah ﷻ in desperation, and then turned away again when relief came. Each exposure to truth that they reject digs their hole deeper.

Ayah 16

يَوْمَ نَبْطِشُ ٱلْبَطْشَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ إِنَّا مُنتَقِمُونَ

“The day We will seize with the greatest seizure - indeed, We will take retribution.” The word بَطْشَة means to grab and squeeze with overwhelming force. This is the ultimate reckoning that awaits those who ridiculed revelation and mocked Allah’s messengers. The vengeance mentioned here is absolute and inevitable.

Ayah 17

۞ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَجَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ now draws a parallel between the Quraysh and another nation that faced similar trials. “We had already tested before them the people of Pharaoh, and there came to them a noble messenger.” The mention of the smoke affecting Makkah connects directly to this narrative, as similar trials were given to Pharaoh through Musa (AS).

Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t even need to name Musa (AS) here - He simply says “a noble messenger” (rasulun kareem). By this point in the Quran’s revelation, the Quraysh have heard so much about Musa (AS) that this description alone is sufficient. No messenger is mentioned more frequently in the Quran than Musa (AS).

Ayah 18

أَنْ أَدُّوٓا۟ إِلَىَّ عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

Musa (AS) delivered his message: “Deliver to me the servants of Allah. Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger.” The Arabic structure here carries two meanings through the particle لَكُمْ . It can mean “I am a messenger sent to you” and also “I am a messenger for your benefit, for you.”

This second meaning is crucial. Throughout history, when messengers came to their people, they were viewed as a nuisance - someone demanding they change their business practices, their speech, their conduct, their entire way of life. The people thought: “This person has lost his mind.” But the messenger came telling them the opposite: “I am actually a favor from Allah ﷻ to you. I am here for your benefit.”

As we learned in the previous surah, one of the great benefits of having the Prophet ﷺ in Makkah was that his presence protected them from destruction. When messengers leave - when Nuh (AS) boards his ship, when Musa (AS) crosses the water - what happens to those towns? They are finished. The fact that the Prophet ﷺ remained among them was a tremendous mercy.

Ayah 19

وَأَن لَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

Musa (AS) continued: “And do not be arrogant with Allah. Indeed, I have come to you with clear authority.” The phrase لَا تَعْلُوا means don’t show your height, don’t try to demonstrate your power or greatness. Don’t attempt to intimidate me with your strength, and certainly don’t try to show arrogance against Allah ﷻ. “I am coming to you with an obvious authority - clear proof from your Master.”

Ayah 20

وَإِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُمْ أَن تَرْجُمُونِ

“And indeed, I have sought refuge in my Master and your Master lest you stone me.” This ayah reveals the specific death penalty Pharaoh had issued against Musa (AS) - death by stoning. Earlier in the Quran, we learned that Pharaoh himself said, “Let me kill Musa.” Now we learn the method he chose.

But Musa (AS) isn’t asking this because he fears death. Messengers don’t worry about that. When Musa (AS) met Allah ﷻ on the mountain, he was already told: إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ - “Indeed, you are from those who are safe.” He knew he wouldn’t be killed. His concern was for them. Attempting to kill a messenger is one of the highest crimes imaginable. By warning them, he was essentially saying: “Your situation is already dire. If you try to come after me, you’re asking for trouble beyond what you can comprehend. You’re not dealing with me - you’re dealing with Allah ﷻ.”

Ayah 21

وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ لِى فَٱعْتَزِلُونِ

“And if you do not believe me, then leave me alone.” This is a final plea for their own benefit. If you won’t accept the message, at least don’t compound your crimes by attempting to harm the messenger.

Ayah 22

فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ مُّجْرِمُونَ

“So he called upon his Master: ‘Indeed, these are a criminal people.’” The most powerful weapon a prophet possesses is his supplication to Allah ﷻ. When he complains to Allah ﷻ, the matter is decided. Notice what the Quraysh are being told: your Prophet ﷺ keeps praying for you, not against you. But there was a noble prophet before who prayed against his nation, and we won’t hold it against this Prophet ﷺ if he does the same, because you deserve it.

Look at the restraint in Musa’s (AS) words. He didn’t say “Master, torture them” or present a lengthy prosecution of their crimes. He simply stated the fact: “These are a criminal people.” That’s all a messenger needs to say. The army of angels stands ready to be unleashed at a moment’s notice.

Ayah 23

فَأَسْرِ بِعِبَادِى لَيْلًا إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ

Allah ﷻ responds: “Set out with My servants by night. Indeed, you will be pursued.” The word لَيْلًا is significant. It doesn’t just mean “at night” - it specifies a particular window of time during the night, a limited opportunity when the guards are elsewhere or at the other end of their patrol route. This is your moment to escape.

How did the Prophet ﷺ leave Makkah? In the middle of the night. How did Musa (AS) leave Egypt? In the middle of the night. And in both cases: إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ - “You will be followed.” The parallels between Musa’s (AS) story and the Prophet’s ﷺ experience are preparing him for what lies ahead.

Ayah 24

وَٱتْرُكِ ٱلْبَحْرَ رَهْوًا ۖ إِنَّهُمْ جُندٌ مُّغْرَقُونَ

“And leave the sea as it is, calm and parted. Indeed, they are an army to be drowned.” This command reveals something remarkable. Musa (AS) was given both the “on switch” and the “off switch.” When he struck his staff, the water parted. Striking it again would close the water. As he reached the other side with the Children of Israel behind him and Pharaoh’s army approaching, logic would dictate: strike the staff again to close the water and protect yourself.

But Allah ﷻ commands: Leave it open. Don’t close it. Leave the door open. Why? إِنَّهُمْ جُندٌ مُّغْرَقُونَ - “They are an army destined to be drowned.”

Ayah 25

كَمْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

“How many gardens and springs did they leave behind.” Allah ﷻ now describes what Pharaoh’s elite forces abandoned. These weren’t ordinary soldiers - they were Pharaoh’s chosen guard. Egypt under the Pharaohs was a military state, and in such states, the military class enjoys elite economic status.

When Pharaoh called his generals to pursue what he viewed as a national threat, he summoned the cream of his army. These were men who lived in luxury, and Allah ﷻ enumerates what they left behind forever.

Ayah 26

وَزُرُوعٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ

“And crops and noble residences.” These beautiful, well-appointed homes belonged to the military elite. In military-dominated states, when someone rises to lieutenant, colonel, or general, they receive not just a nice house but servants, estates, and every luxury. This was the life Pharaoh’s forces enjoyed - and lost forever in a single day.

Ayah 27

وَنَعْمَةٍ كَانُوا۟ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهِينَ

“And comfort wherein they were amused.” The word فَاكِهِينَ means overjoyed, smiling, relaxing - people who lived in complete ease and luxury, enjoying every comfort without a care.

Ayah 28

كَذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ

“Thus it was. And We caused to inherit it another people.” This is how Allah ﷻ operates. Their wealth, their land, their entire legacy was transferred to another nation altogether. Everything they accumulated, everything they built, everything they took pride in - gone in an instant.

Ayah 29

فَمَا بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنظَرِينَ

“And the heaven and earth did not weep for them, nor were they reprieved.” This striking imagery teaches us something profound. The scholars derive from this ayah that when the righteous die, the sky and the earth do cry for them. But for people like Pharaoh’s army - oppressors, criminals, rejecters of truth - even creation itself does not mourn their passing.

وَمَا كَانُوا مُنظَرِينَ - “They were not given any additional time.” No delay, no second chance, no reprieve. Their time was up.

Ayah 30

وَلَقَدْ نَجَّيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ

“And We certainly saved the Children of Israel from the humiliating punishment.” Allah ﷻ rescued them from a torment that was not just painful but degrading - a punishment that stripped away human dignity.

Ayah 31

مِن فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَالِيًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ

“From Pharaoh. Indeed, he was a haughty one among the transgressors.” The description here is precise: عَالِيًا مِّنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ - “high up among those who commit excess.” Everything Pharaoh did, he did in excess. When he built a palace, he built it in excess even if it meant killing thousands of slaves. When he feared an uprising, he ordered the systematic slaughter of Israelite boys every other year. Every action, every decision was characterized by transgression beyond bounds.

Ayah 32

وَلَقَدِ ٱخْتَرْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

“And We certainly chose them by knowledge over the worlds.” This ayah addresses a crucial question: If Allah ﷻ already knew the Children of Israel would fail - that they would worship the calf, insult messengers, reject prophets like Isa (AS) - why choose them at all? Why give them special status despite knowing their flaws?

The answer operates on two levels. First, regarding the truly wicked, Allah ﷻ allows them to dig their hole deeper. More exposure to truth means greater accountability. But second, there’s a worldly parallel that helps us understand: Imagine a parent with an extremely intelligent child who simply won’t apply themselves. The parent enrolls them in an elite school, knowing that with even a little effort, they could excel. But the child doesn’t try and fails. The parent gives them another chance, and another, and another - not out of foolishness, but because the potential for greatness is undeniable.

The Children of Israel were chosen because they were an incredible nation. This is not about insulting them - we must recognize their remarkable qualities. They received messenger after messenger after messenger. Despite all the attacks on their scriptures, despite conquering nations attempting to erase their identity, despite their books being burned and their communities scattered, they maintained the core elements of revelation for thousands of years. This required extraordinary scholarly dedication.

In Surah Al-Baqarah, Allah ﷻ says: إِنِّي فَضَّلْتُكُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ - “I have preferred you over all other nations.” Why? So they could be a role model nation. This is what a nation under divine guidance should look like: justice, benevolence, mercy, guidance for humanity, love for humanity. The potential was always there.

Unlike other nations that faced one messenger and then were destroyed when they rejected him, the Israelites were given chance after chance after chance. This itself was the preference - the opportunity to fulfill their potential despite their repeated failures.

Ayah 33

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ مَا فِيهِ بَلَـٰٓؤٌا۟ مُّبِينٌ

“And We gave them of signs that in which there was a clear trial.” The miracles given to the Israelites and to Pharaoh’s people served dual purposes - they were opportunities to believe, but they were also clear tests. Each sign was both guidance and examination.

Ayah 34-35

إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَيَقُولُونَ • إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُنشَرِينَ

The focus now returns to the Quraysh. “Indeed, these people are saying: ‘There is nothing but our first death, and we will not be resurrected.’” After hearing the entire story of Pharaoh’s destruction, after experiencing the smoke themselves, after witnessing miracle upon miracle in the Quran, they still persist in their denial of resurrection.

The word مُنشَرِينَ comes from نَشْر meaning to raise and spread. They’re saying: “Once we die and mix with the earth, we won’t be raised up and spread out again. There’s no such thing as resurrection.”

Ayah 36

فَأْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

They challenge the believers: “Then bring back our forefathers, if you are truthful.” If there’s any truth to your claims about life after death, they demand, let’s see our ancestors now. Why wait until the Day of Judgment?

Ayah 37

أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ responds to their arrogance: “Are they better or the people of Tubba’ and those before them? We destroyed them. Indeed, they were criminals.” The people of Tubba’ were the powerful kings of Yemen to the south of Makkah. The Quraysh knew their might firsthand - they had witnessed the incident when these kings attempted to invade Makkah and destroy the Kaaba, only to be destroyed by Allah ﷻ Himself.

The parallel is devastating: You know how powerful Tubba’ and his people were. You know that only Allah ﷻ saved you from them. Yet you dare to think you’re immune to His judgment? We destroyed them despite their power, and they were criminals just like those who reject the truth today.

Ayah 38

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ

“And We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in play.” This ayah confronts a dangerous philosophical notion - the idea that God created the universe as some kind of game or amusement, that He toys with creation out of boredom, throwing in floods here, earthquakes there, crashing planes, having His entertainment with us.

Allah ﷻ categorically rejects this. Creation has purpose. The universe operates according to divine wisdom, not divine whim. Every test, every trial, every moment of ease and difficulty serves a meaningful purpose in a reality that will culminate in the Day of Judgment, where perfect justice will be established.

Ayah 38–59 · linked video: 03. Ad-Dukhan (Ayah 38-59)

Surah Ad-Dukhan: Ayahs 38-59

Ayah 38

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ

Allah ﷻ responds here to a deeply misguided notion about creation. Some belief systems, including certain dimensions of Hindu creed, suggest that God plays with humanity like a child plays with toys—toying with creation without wisdom or purpose. This is nothing short of blasphemy.

There are those who say, “Sure, maybe there’s a God. I’m ready to believe that. But why is He playing around with us? Why is He doing this?” This ayah directly refutes such thinking. We didn’t create the heavens and the earth and everything between them as if We were playing around. The Arabic word لَـٰعِبِينَ conveys the image of someone engaged in purposeless play, and Allah ﷻ categorically denies this characterization of His creation.

Ayah 39

مَا خَلَقْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

We didn’t create them at all except بِٱلْحَقِّ —with purpose, with truth. All of these things were created to meet a goal. However, most of them don’t even know. Most of humanity remains unaware of the profound purpose embedded in creation.

The purpose of the skies and the earth is to eventually wind down and present themselves before Allah ﷻ for the Day of Judgment—the day of the final decision making.

Ayah 40

إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ مِيقَـٰتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

The Day of الْفَصْلِ —the final separation and judgment—is مِيقَـٰتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ , their appointed time for all of them. The word مِيقَـٰت refers to a powerful, momentous time—the great time when all of creation will stand before its Creator.

Ayah 41

يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى مَوْلًى عَن مَّوْلًى شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

This is the day on which no مَوْلًى —no powerful friend, no protecting friend—is going to aid any other protecting friend in any way, shape or form. وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ —and they’re not the ones that are going to be aided.

Ayah 42

إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

Except the one that Allah ﷻ would show mercy and love to. Certainly He is الْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ —the ultimate authority, the One who always shows love and mercy.

Ayahs 43-50

إِنَّ شَجَرَتَ ٱلزَّقُّومِ • طَعَامُ ٱلْأَثِيمِ • كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَغْلِى فِى ٱلْبُطُونِ • كَغَلْىِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ • خُذُوهُ فَٱعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ • ثُمَّ صُبُّوا۟ فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ • ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْكَرِيمُ • إِنَّ هَـٰذَا مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ

Now that Allah ﷻ mentions His mercy, out of that very mercy He describes Hellfire. This is part of our belief system—it’s out of His mercy that He describes Hellfire in such detail. Why? It’s the scared straight program in the Quran.

Hell is not just described as a place that’s bad. It’s very graphic. It’s laid out with tremendous detail because the Arabic language is so picturesque, so rich with imagery, that you can visualize these intense scenes. The point is to terrify you enough that you get your act together. If Hellfire wasn’t described in such depth and with such graphic language, then why would anyone take it seriously? How bad could it be?

Consider the concept of hell in modern entertainment. What has it become? It’s a joke. People wear jackets that say “Highway to Hell” with flaming skulls. Cartoons are made featuring characters from hell. Action figures exist of superheroes that supposedly come from hell, or stories of people journeying into hell and breaking right back out like it’s just a prison you can escape from. It’s no big deal anymore. It’s become entertainment—fun horror stories to amuse yourself with.

But this is a reality: إِنَّ شَجَرَتَ ٱلزَّقُّومِ —the tree of Zaqqum, that’s its proper name, one of the trees of Hellfire. طَعَامُ ٱلْأَثِيمِ )—it is the food of the one constantly engrossed in sin. The word أَثِيمِ describes someone perpetually steeped in wrongdoing.

كَٱلْمُهْلِ —it’s like boiling hot water. المُهْل is also used to describe blood. يَغْلِى فِى ٱلْبُطُونِ —it sizzles in the belly. You know how when you take oil and pour it on a pan that’s already hot, it makes that sizzling sound? That’s the يَغْلِى (. It gets into the belly and then it starts sizzling inside. كَغَلْىِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ —like the scorching and sizzling of boiling water when it reaches its peak temperature.

خُذُوهُ —grab him! فَٱعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ —and shove him inside, into the very middle of the Hellfire, into the roaring flame. ثُمَّ صُبُّوا۟ فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ then pour above his head from the punishment of boiling hot water. There’s torture going on inside of him—internally he’s sizzling—and on top of him too.

ذُقْ —taste this! إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْكَرِيمُ —you’re the one, right? You’re the one that has the ultimate authority, the noble one! This is biting sarcasm directed at those who were drunk on their own status. Remember, one of the great reasons the people of Makkah didn’t accept the Prophet’s ﷺ message is that Allah ﷻ describes them as too intoxicated with their own greatness and status—they were مُطْرَفِينَ, those who lived luxuriously and arrogantly. Here’s what their nobility amounts to: taste this. This is what your arrogance was worth.

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ —this is the very thing you used to be in doubt about.

Ayahs 51-57

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ • فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ • يَلْبَسُونَ مِن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ • كَذَٰلِكَ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ • يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ ءَامِنِينَ • لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ إِلَّا ٱلْمَوْتَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ • فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

No doubt about it—those who sought to protect themselves, the righteous المُتَّقِينَ , are going to be فِى مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ—in a safe place, a secure station.

فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ —in gardens with flowing springs surrounding them. يَلْبَسُونَ مِن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ —they’re going to be dressing in sundus and istabraq, light silk and heavy silk. They can have two different kinds of silk garments. مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ —meeting one another, facing each other. First you get dressed, you pick your outfit, then you go hang out with other people in Paradise. “Hey, what are you doing here?” Just facing one another, enjoying each other’s company.

كَذَٰلِكَ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ —that is how We reward, and We will have married them off to حُورٍ عِينٍ —beautiful spouses. The word عِينٍ refers to eyes that are hypnotic, captivating. It’s when the white of the eyes is really white and the dark of the eyes is really dark—the contrast in the eyes is just mesmerizing. This is what حُورٍ عِينٍ describes.

يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ —they’re going to be calling for all kinds of fruits. ءَامِنِينَ —sitting peacefully, relaxed. They’re just ordering food: “Hey, what do you want to eat? Let’s have that food we had.” They’re tasting, and we’ve discussed the concept of taste before. They’re just there, relaxed in Jannah.

لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ إِلَّا ٱلْمَوْتَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ —they’re not going to be tasting death except the first death. This is actually profound sarcasm in the Quran. The disbelievers used to say, “We’re not going to have death except the first death—we die once and that’s it.” Allah ﷻ says, “Yes, first death—that’s only for these people. They’re only going to be able to say that the first death we already tasted is the only one we’re going to taste.”

And by the way, for the person in Hellfire, you could argue, “Well, they’re not going to taste death either, right?” Actually, yes they are. Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, لَا يُقْضَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا —“Don’t ask for death today. Ask for death over and over again.” They will experience the tortures of death one after the other, continually. That’s what التَّوُّد in Arabic conveys. So on the one hand, there is no death at all in Paradise, and on the other, there is continual death in the Fire, one wave after another.

وَوَقَىٰهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ —and He had protected them from the punishment of the roaring flame. فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكَ —this is out of a great favor from your Master. ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ —that, in fact, is the ultimate success.

Ayah 58

فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ

فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ —then We have only made it easy on your tongue. لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ —We’ve only done that so that they can benefit from the advice, so they can remember.

Consider philosophers with their big words and complex ideas. The higher up you go intellectually—someone with a PhD in literature, philosophy, or sociology—the less able they often become to communicate with regular people. They’re no longer able to talk to a teenager because they’re way up there with their specialized knowledge. They can’t tone it down. The more knowledge you accumulate, and you’ll notice this with scholars too (with few exceptions), when scholars reach a certain level of scholarship, they’re sometimes unable to speak to the masses anymore. When they open their mouths, the floodgates of knowledge pour out, the faucet can’t be turned off, and people get overwhelmed: “Oh my God, he knows so much, and I don’t understand most of what he’s talking about.”

Who knows more than Allah ﷻ? And Allah ﷻ is revealing this Book. This Book could have been so much more complicated. But one of the designs of this Book, one of its great qualities, one of its powers, is that Allah ﷻ made it easy on your tongue. Not just that it’s easy for the Prophet ﷺ to recite—that’s one dimension—but He made the language itself easy, understandable, simple. It’s not complicated.

وَالْعَصْرِ إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ —“By time, indeed humanity is in loss.” It’s not complicated. It’s straightforward.

لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ —so they can just try to remember, they can try to take the advice. And for Muslims, there’s another added benefit: Allah ﷻ made it easy on your tongue so that they can make an effort to memorize. It’s another meaning of يَتَذَكَّرُونَ here—so they can memorize.

Just get started. You’ll be shocked how easy it is. You just never thought about it. You never thought you could do it. Just do it. Try one or two ayahs today. Just begin. “But I don’t have a teacher. I don’t know. I might read it imperfectly.” Stop making excuses. Just do it. Just start memorizing. Work on your Tajweed—I’m not saying don’t work on it—but memorize, and make that a part of your life.

On the days when you memorize Quran, you’ll notice something about those days that you don’t notice on days you didn’t memorize. You’ll testify to this yourself.

Ayah 59

فَٱرْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُم مُّرْتَقِبُونَ

فَٱرْتَقِبْ —you wait in anticipation. You wait anxiously. إِنَّهُم مُّرْتَقِبُونَ —because they, those disbelievers, are also waiting anxiously, waiting in anticipation.
